findings.title: Golden Ratio: 60 DMs/day for 30 days guarantees first client
findings.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
findings.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no codified daily volume target or tracking cadence (§6). No 'golden ratio' equivalent exists internally — outreach volume is ad hoc.
findings.recommendation: Codify a minimum daily outreach quota (e.g., 60 DMs/day) for whoever owns acquisition at Palm. Build or adopt a simple lead tracker (Airtable view or spreadsheet) to log status and follow-ups, matching the systematic volume this competitor prescribes.

sources.quote: For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

findings.title: Cross-platform username matching: find OF creators on X, then locate on IG
findings.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
findings.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented prospect-sourcing method beyond Josh's personal DMs and referrals (§6). No cross-platform lookup or IG graph-traversal tactic is codified.
findings.recommendation: Document a prospect-sourcing SOP using both methods: (1) X keyword search → IG username lookup, and (2) IG related-accounts traversal from any known OF creator. This is low-cost and immediately actionable for whoever runs outreach at Palm.

sources.quote: you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username

findings.title: First DM: short curiosity-piquing message focused on a specific observable problem
findings.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
findings.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message framework or script (§6 GAP). First-contact approach is entirely at Josh's discretion.
findings.recommendation: Create a first-DM template library: short (1–3 lines), profile-specific observation + curiosity hook, no agency pitch. Test from a personal or agency IG account. This is a direct complement to Palm's existing pipeline — once a creator is signed the content engine (§1) kicks in immediately.

sources.quote: focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio

findings.title: Google Drive onboarding folder: info sheet + logins + pre-made content scripts
findings.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
findings.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.
findings.vs_us: Palm's onboarding collects name, email, commission %, state, contract, and voice memo via a form wizard (§6), but has no documented model info sheet for chatters, no platform-login collection protocol, and no pre-made content script intake. Communication boundary-setting is not formalized.
findings.recommendation: Add three items to Palm's onboarding wizard or a linked Google Drive template: (1) a model info sheet with personal details for the chatting team, (2) a platform-login collection step, (3) a pre-made content upload prompt. Also add a written communication expectations section (channel, hours) to the onboarding flow to reduce creator churn friction.

sources.quote: within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts

findings.title: Assign each creator a hobby-based niche to give the algorithm a clear target audience
findings.claim: Choosing a specific non-adult hobby niche for a creator (e.g., fishing, golf) allows social media algorithms to categorize and distribute content to an engaged audience, dramatically increasing viral reach compared to generic dance/lifestyle posts.
findings.what_they_do: Agency identifies a real hobby the creator has, then builds all social content around that niche. The algorithm can categorize the content, show it to an existing interested community, and that community's engagement triggers viral distribution. Paid shoutouts on niche pages also become cheaper and more targeted. Creator generates content organically during activities she already does.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong on data-driven ideation from viral inspo reels but does not document a niche-assignment step per creator. The system scrapes what is already viral broadly; it does not codify the step of choosing one thematic niche per creator to anchor the algorithm.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'niche selection' step to Palm's creator onboarding and content pipeline (§1, step 5 or pre-step). For each creator, define one or two content niches based on their genuine interests. Feed that niche as a filter into the Apify scrape / Inspo Sources targeting so viral reference reels are pulled from that niche's top performers, not just broadly popular content.

sources.quote: if you find a niche for your model, that will make the social media's job a lot easier to find the target audience

findings.title: Escalating PPV price ladder: free photo → $15 → $50 → $100 → $150 → $200
findings.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP, scripts, or pricing ladder documented. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team roadmap and the AI chatting project.
findings.recommendation: Capture this escalating PPV ladder as the foundational script template for Palm's future in-house chat team. Specifically: define free-teaser → low-anchor PPV → mid-tier PPV → high-tier PPV price points; have creators pre-shoot content sequences in order before chatters launch; use South Africa as primary chatter sourcing market for English-speaking creator accounts. Brief the external chat team on whether they already use this structure.

sources.quote: the price goes from $15 to 50, then to 100, then to $150, then to $200, and before you know it, the person has spent maybe $300, $400

findings.title: Do chatting yourself for 2 weeks before hiring, then hire South African chatters for cultural fluency
findings.claim: Agency owners should personally do DM chatting for two weeks to understand fan psychology and upsell mechanics before hiring chatters, and should prioritize South African hires over Southeast Asian for native English slang and cultural alignment.
findings.what_they_do: New agency operators personally handle all DMs for roughly two weeks to learn what works. They then hire chatters from South Africa (preferred) or South America, paying in local currency (favorable exchange rate). Three chatters per creator account rotate 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage. Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting entirely today (§8); this tactic is build-toward for the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap). No internal SOP exists for chatter hiring geography or shift structure.
findings.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, use this framework: (1) have the team lead personally chat for 2 weeks per creator to develop training materials, (2) prioritize South African candidates for English-language creator accounts, (3) structure 3-chatter rotating 8-hour shifts per active creator account. Document this in the chat-team hiring SOP before recruitment begins.

sources.quote: step two, is to hire chatters... the vast majority of our team is from SA, from South Africa. Mainly because English is their first language

findings.title: Position the agency offer around creator outcomes (time saved, revenue growth), not the 50% split
findings.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
findings.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or offer framing (§7 GAP). Commission % is set per-creator at onboarding with no stated rationale or tiering. This is Palm's #1 identified gap.
findings.recommendation: Write Palm's standard offer narrative: lead with the two pain points (fan growth, time), show a concrete growth trajectory from an existing client, and frame the 50% split as a performance-aligned partnership rather than a fee. Consider creating a one-page 'offer doc' with revenue-growth evidence that can be shared during acquisition outreach.

sources.quote: what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

findings.title: Use Guaranteed Gains to buy a fixed subscriber count from larger pages
findings.claim: Paying a larger creator or agency a flat fee to guarantee a specific number of new free fans is a cost-effective cold-traffic channel, especially for trans pages.
findings.what_they_do: Agency pays a page owner a fixed dollar amount (example: ~$140 for 500 GGs or 1,000 GGs) to post the new creator's page plus send a mass DM to their fan list, guaranteeing at least that many new free subscribers. ROI is tracked as: fans acquired × LTV minus cost. Example given: 200 GGs at $500 cost, $11 LTV = $2,200 revenue, $1,700 profit. Deals are sourced through OFM Telegram groups and tracked in a spreadsheet. A dedicated 'queue manager' role schedules and verifies every promo fires on time, creates tracking links, and runs profit/loss analysis per promo to identify which partners to scale.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic or paid-distribution strategy (§5 GAP). No equivalent of guaranteed-gains deal-sourcing, queue-manager role, or per-promo ROI tracking exists in the Palm baseline.
findings.recommendation: Build a lightweight GG tracking sheet (partner, cost, fans guaranteed, fans delivered, LTV estimate, profit) even before activating this channel. For real creators in fetish-adjacent niches, test one GG deal with a Telegram-sourced partner at a small budget ($200–$500) to validate LTV assumptions. Appoint or assign queue-manager responsibility to an existing ops role so timing and compliance don't slip.

sources.quote: I will give you 200 of my fans guaranteed for 500 bucks
queue managers are creating tracking links and running profit analysis on every single promo

findings.title: Build a Telegram-sourced S4S network before signing creators to pre-load launch traffic
findings.claim: Developing a network of same-size trans creators and agencies for mutual shoutouts on OF, IG, Telegram, TikTok, and X before launch allows an agency to spike a new creator to tens of thousands of fans on day one.
findings.what_they_do: Agency owner joins as many OFM Telegram groups as possible, posts availability for S4S and GG collabs, and records all contacts in a spreadsheet. Matching logic: pages of roughly equal fan count do S4S (mutual shoutout); significantly larger pages are approached for paid GGs instead. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules promos from the entire network so that on launch day the new creator immediately receives a surge of fans. Once internal pages grow, new creator launches are boosted by the agency's own portfolio of existing creators ('internal boosting'), eliminating the need to pay for early GGs.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented S4S or network-building playbook (§5 GAP). The system is entirely absent from Palm's current ops baseline.
findings.recommendation: Assign a part-time 'network getter' task (could be Josh or a VA) to join 5–10 OFM Telegram groups and begin logging trans-creator and agency contacts into an Airtable table (Name, Platform, Fan Count, Contact Method, Last Outreach, Deal Type). Even if Palm doesn't operate trans creators today, the mechanics of S4S and launch-day pre-scheduling are transferable to any niche and should be piloted on the next real-creator launch.

sources.quote: you're going to gain most of your network through Telegram groups
when you onboard new creators, you pre-schedule promos from your entire network and all of your internal pages as well, boosting new pages to $100,000 in the first month

findings.title: Separate 'network getter' and 'queue manager' roles to operationalize promotion at scale
findings.claim: A $100K/month promo machine requires two distinct ops roles: one that expands the contact network daily and one that schedules, tracks, and enforces timing on every active promotion deal.
findings.what_they_do: Network getters are in Telegram groups every day adding new creator and agency contacts. Queue managers hold the schedule of every live S4S and GG deal, confirm partners post on time, create unique tracking links per promo, and run profit analysis to identify high-ROI partners for reinvestment. A separate media buying team handles paid IG meme-page buys. Reddit/X contractors distribute content as an additional traffic stream. All roles are concurrent and ongoing, not launch-only.
findings.vs_us: Palm has Amin for manual posting and an external chat team but no equivalent network-getter or queue-manager role (§10 THIN). Traffic strategy is an explicit GAP (§5).
findings.recommendation: When Palm is ready to activate a distribution playbook, define these two roles explicitly in the org chart before hiring. In the short term, create a simple Airtable table ('Promo Queue') with fields: Partner, Deal Type, Cost, Scheduled Date, Confirmed Posted (Y/N), Tracking Link, Fans Delivered, Profit. This infrastructure costs nothing to build now and makes the queue-manager role immediately hirable when needed.

sources.quote: your network getters are reaching out to new leads and creators every day expanding the size of your network
your queue managers are constantly in combo with all of your network arranging shout out for shout out and GGs

findings.topic: Reddit and X as supplementary traffic channels
findings.title: Contract out Reddit and X content distribution as a low-overhead traffic stream
findings.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
findings.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit or X traffic strategy (§5 GAP). This is a pure learn-from-others gap that applies to real creators and is relatively easy to test at low cost.
findings.recommendation: Source a Reddit/X contractor via OFM Telegram groups. Start with a 30-day paid pilot (~$500–$1K) for one creator, using UTM-tagged links or OF referral tracking to attribute revenue. If 5x+ ROI is confirmed, formalize as a recurring line item per creator.

sources.quote: I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit

findings.title: Gate paid cold-traffic spend on known LTV; only scale if margins are confirmed
findings.claim: The decision to run VA-led cold outreach or any paid cold-traffic campaign should be gated on whether the creator's LTV is high enough to make the acquisition cost profitable.
findings.what_they_do: Before activating VA cold-outreach or media buying, the agency calculates the estimated LTV per free fan (example used: $11/fan) and sets a maximum allowable acquisition cost per fan based on a target margin (example: willing to pay up to $5,000 for 500 GGs = $10/fan, expecting $11 LTV). Every promo deal is evaluated against this threshold before committing budget. Tactics that don't meet the margin threshold are not activated regardless of how low-cost they appear.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented LTV per fan figure, no PPV pricing model, and no acquisition-cost threshold (§7 GAP, §6 GAP). Without these numbers, Palm cannot evaluate any paid traffic channel rationally.
findings.recommendation: As a prerequisite to any paid traffic or GG experimentation, calculate LTV per free fan for at least one existing creator using historical OF data (total revenue ÷ total free fans over a rolling 90-day window). Store this in Airtable per creator. Even a rough number unlocks rational spend decisions across all traffic channels.

sources.quote: cold traffic lead generation with VAs outreaching to cold audience and converting them — if your LTV is high enough, this will make sense
I am going to be willing to pay upwards of $5,000 for 500 GGs

findings.topic: Pre-launch network building before signing creators
findings.title: Build distribution network of 10–20 partner creators before signing your first creator in a niche
findings.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
findings.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led and reactive (§6 THIN). There is no documented pre-signing network-building step or launch-day traffic playbook. Palm typically figures out distribution after a creator is onboarded.
findings.recommendation: For any new niche Palm enters (including trans if that becomes a focus), add a pre-launch checklist step: minimum N partner contacts secured in Telegram before creator onboarding begins. Document required contacts and deal commitments as a gate in the creator onboarding flow, making distribution readiness a precondition of launch rather than an afterthought.

sources.quote: before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in

findings.title: Choose fetish niches (e.g. trans) over mainstream niches to unlock paid traffic channels and reduce parasocial dependency
findings.claim: Trans and fetish-adjacent niches are structurally easier to scale to $100K/month because fans are content-driven rather than parasocial-relationship-driven, making cold-traffic channels like GGs and media buying viable without a warm-up funnel.
findings.what_they_do: Agency deliberately targets fetish-driven niches where the fan–creator relationship is transactional. In these niches, cold traffic from meme pages, GGs, and S4S converts directly to paying subscribers without needing a Cupid-style warm-up sequence required for mainstream (cis) creators. This structural difference means the traffic stack is simpler, cheaper, and more scalable. The operator cites knowing multiple agencies doing million-dollar profit months exclusively via trans agencies.
findings.vs_us: Palm's niche strategy is not documented (§6 GAP, §7 GAP). Palm currently uses Cupid-style warming for cis creators (referenced in transcript as the workaround for cold traffic not converting). The structural point — that fetish niches eliminate the warm-up dependency — is a strategic insight Palm should weigh when evaluating creator acquisition targets.
findings.recommendation: When Palm evaluates new creator signings, add a niche-type field to the creator intake process (fetish vs. mainstream) and document the expected traffic stack per type. If Palm experiments with trans or other fetish creators, expect that GG and S4S channels will convert more efficiently and that the warm-up content requirement (§1 pipeline) can be reduced or replaced with a faster paid-traffic playbook.

sources.quote: I know a couple guys in the OFM space that are doing million-dollar months — they're doing it with a trans agency
in the trans community, people that are into this type of stuff don't care about the creator — they just want to get their hands on as much as they possibly can

findings.title: Set hard minimum PPV prices: $19.99 images, $39.99 videos, $300 customs
findings.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
findings.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or price floors (§7, explicit GAP). No internal baseline exists for what creators should charge for images, videos, or customs.
findings.recommendation: Establish Palm's standard PPV price floor as agency policy: ~$20 minimum for images, ~$40 minimum for videos, ~$300 for customs. Document this in the creator onboarding materials and share with the external chat team as a standing instruction. This is a zero-cost, immediate revenue lever.

sources.quote: you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

findings.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send
findings.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
findings.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.
findings.vs_us: No Palm-side pricing ladder or escalation logic exists (§7 GAP). The external chat team sets its own approach; Palm has no documented position on warm-up pricing or escalation sequencing.
findings.recommendation: Define a two-phase pricing ladder for the external chat team and future in-house chatters: Phase 1 (new sub warm-up) = first video at ~$35; Phase 2 (warmed fan) = $40+ escalating. Write this into the chat team brief and eventually into Palm's in-house chatter SOP (§8 build-toward).

sources.quote: it will sell them a video for $34.99 yes it is slightly cheaper than the $40 I mentioned earlier but this is to warm them up
once they purchase this $34.99 video then you can consider them warmed up and then whenever you sell them a piece of content after that it is going to be at least 40 and upwards

findings.title: Never sell PPV packs or bundles — sell individual pieces only
findings.claim: Selling content in packs commoditizes it and undermines the per-item price anchoring that drives high revenue per fan.
findings.what_they_do: The agency explicitly bans 'packs' as a pricing format. All PPV is sold as individual items at or above the price floors. The reasoning is that packs reframe the value proposition toward volume/discount, which is the same psychological trap as low per-item pricing.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented stance on packs vs. individual PPV pricing (§7 GAP). No internal policy exists.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'no packs' rule to the Palm pricing policy alongside the price floors. Instruct the external chat team accordingly. When building the in-house chat SOP, codify individual-item-only PPV selling as a default.

sources.quote: you especially don't want to sell packs no packs

findings.title: Require upfront tips before producing any custom content to prevent rip-offs
findings.claim: Fans will request custom content and then disappear after delivery, so requiring tips before production is non-negotiable.
findings.what_they_do: For custom content ($300+), the agency requires the fan to tip multiple times before the creator starts filming. The rationale is to assume all fans will attempt to get content without paying, so payment must be front-loaded. No tip = no custom.
findings.vs_us: No Palm policy on custom content fulfillment order exists (§7 GAP, §8 OUTSOURCED). The external chat team handles this ad hoc.
findings.recommendation: Add a customs fulfillment rule to the chat team brief: minimum $300 for customs, all payment (or at least a meaningful deposit tip) collected before any production begins. Document this in the future in-house chatter SOP.

sources.quote: they're going to tip you multiple times before you even start cuz otherwise you're going to make it and they're just going to rip you off

findings.title: Deploy an automated new-subscriber script that creates a scenario and sells a first PPV at ~$35
findings.claim: An automated message sent to every new subscriber that creates a conversational scenario and pitches a first video purchase converts cold new fans into paying customers before a live chatter has to engage.
findings.what_they_do: A pre-written automated script goes to every new sub immediately after they subscribe. It creates a light interactive scenario (not specified in detail), leads the fan through a short interaction, and closes with a $34.99 video offer. Once the fan buys, they are flagged as 'warmed up' and handed to regular chat flow at full price floors.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). No automated new-subscriber script is documented on the Palm side. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap.
findings.recommendation: For the AI chatting project and in-house chat roadmap (§8): design a standardized new-subscriber automation that immediately engages the fan, creates a light scenario, and attempts a ~$35 first PPV sale. This de-risks the cold-fan problem and reduces reliance on live chatter time for conversion.

sources.quote: it will send to the sub it will create a little scenario it will get them to do something and then it will sell them a video for $34.99

findings.title: Remove all visible price menus from the OF bio — menus make the page feel like an eBay store
findings.claim: Displaying a price menu in the OF bio immediately cheapens the creator's brand and signals transactionality before any relationship is built.
findings.what_they_do: When taking over a creator account, the first thing this agency removes is the bio price menu (e.g. '$5 photos, $10 packs'). No prices are listed publicly. Pricing is handled exclusively inside DM conversations after rapport is established, which allows for dynamic escalation and avoids anchoring fans to low numbers.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented OF page setup or bio standards (§7 GAP, §2 THIN). No internal policy on whether price menus should or should not appear in the bio.
findings.recommendation: Establish a Palm account setup standard: no price menus in the OF bio. Add this to the creator onboarding checklist and to the brief given to the external chat team when taking over an account.

sources.quote: the first thing I see when I take over a models only fans account are all these offers something like this there'll be a menu in the bio
as soon as guys subscribe to the only fans account they see this and it feels like an eBay store

findings.topic: CRM for fan spend tracking to guide PPV pricing
findings.title: Use a fan CRM (e.g. Inflow) to read spending habits and calibrate per-fan PPV price escalation
findings.claim: A CRM that surfaces each fan's historical spend enables chatters to know exactly how far to push pricing on any individual fan rather than guessing.
findings.what_they_do: The agency uses a CRM tool called 'Inflow' that tracks per-subscriber spending history. Chatters consult this before sending a PPV to decide at what price point to pitch — fans who have spent more get higher asks. The principle is always to move price upward based on observed willingness to pay.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a Whale Hunting tool and Fan Tracker CRM (§9, PARTIAL) that tracks fan data. However, Palm's CRM is primarily a leadership/reporting layer; there is no documented policy for chatters to use fan spend data to calibrate real-time PPV pricing. The external chat team likely does not have structured access to Palm's fan intelligence.
findings.recommendation: Bridge the §9 fan intelligence system to the external chat team: share per-fan spend summaries so chatters can price escalate based on data rather than guesswork. For the in-house chat roadmap, build a spend-aware pricing prompt directly into the chatter workflow.

sources.quote: if you're using a CRM a customer relationship manager like inflow then those things are going to show you the spending habits for Subs spending habits and then based on these spending habits it shows then you can only go upwards from there
